tracks.name: JavaScript — First Steps
tracks.description: Start from zero. Print, store, compare, branch.

lessons.title: 1. Hello, console
lessons.summary: How JavaScript prints things.

teaching.text: Programs talk to you by printing. In JavaScript, the built-in printer is `console.log()`.
Whatever you pass between the parentheses lands in the Console. Text goes in quotes — single `'…'` or double `"…"`, either works.

console.log('Hi there')
// → Hi there
Lines that start with `//` are comments. JavaScript ignores them; they're notes for humans.

lessons.task: Make the program print exactly: Hello, World!
lessons.starterCode: // Use console.log to print Hello, World!
hints: Call `console.log()` with a string in quotes.
Inside the quotes write exactly: Hello, World! — capital H, capital W, the comma, the exclamation mark.
lessons.solution: console.log('Hello, World!');

lessons.title: 2. Variables with let
lessons.summary: Storing a value under a name you can reuse.

teaching.text: A variable is a labelled box. You put a value in it, then refer to the value by the label.
Read it as: 'let the name `score` mean `10` from now on.' `let` is the keyword that introduces a new variable, the `=` is assignment (not equality).
Once a variable exists, you can change it later: `score = 20;` (no second `let` — that would be a new variable).

lessons.task: Create a variable named `score`, set it to 42, then print it.
lessons.starterCode:
// Declare a variable named score with the value 42
// Then print score
lessons.solution:
let score = 42;
console.log(score);

lessons.title: 3. const vs let
lessons.summary: Names that aren't supposed to change.

teaching.text: Use `const` when the value behind a name should never be reassigned. Use `let` when it should.
Rule of thumb: reach for `const` first. Switch to `let` only when you actually need to reassign.

lessons.task: Declare `const pi = 3.14;` and `let radius = 5;`. Print the area of the circle (pi × radius × radius).
lessons.starterCode: // const pi, let radius — then print the area
hints: Area of a circle is pi * radius * radius.

lessons.title: 4. Numbers and math
lessons.summary: +, -, *, /, % — and the order they run in.

teaching.text: JavaScript has the usual five math operators: `+` add, `-` subtract, `*` multiply, `/` divide, and `%` remainder (the leftover after dividing).

console.log(2 + 3);  // → 5
console.log(10 / 4); // → 2.5
console.log(10 % 3); // → 1   (10 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 1)
Parentheses force the order, exactly like school maths. `(3 + 4) * 2` is `14`, but `3 + 4 * 2` is `11` because `*` runs before `+`.

lessons.task: Print the result of (3 + 4) × 2 − 1 using a single console.log.
lessons.solution: console.log((3 + 4) * 2 - 1);

lessons.title: 5. Strings and template literals
lessons.summary: Stitching variables into text without sticky tape.

teaching.text: Strings are text. You can glue them together with `+`, but it gets ugly: `'Hi, ' + name + '!'`.
Backtick strings — *template literals* — let you drop variables straight in with `${…}`:
Those are backticks `` ` ``, not single quotes. On most keyboards they live to the left of the `1` key.

lessons.task: Store the name 'Sam' in a variable, then print: Hi, Sam!
lessons.starterCode:
// let name = 'Sam';
// Print: Hi, Sam!  (use a template literal)
hints: Wrap the whole greeting in backticks ` ` ` `, not quotes.
lessons.solution:
let name = 'Sam';
console.log(`Hi, ${name}!`);

lessons.title: 6. Booleans and comparisons
lessons.summary: Yes-or-no values that decisions are built on.

teaching.text: Booleans have exactly two values: `true` and `false`. Comparisons produce them.

console.log(7 > 5);     // → true
console.log(3 === 4);   // → false
console.log(3 !== 4);   // → true
Use three equals `===` to check equality and `!==` to check inequality. (`==` exists too but it does weird type-juggling — avoid it.)

lessons.task: Print whether 7 is greater than 5.
lessons.starterCode: // Print the result of: 7 > 5
lessons.solution: console.log(7 > 5);

lessons.title: 7. if / else
lessons.summary: Doing different things based on a condition.

teaching.text: An `if` block runs only when its condition is `true`. An `else` block runs when it's `false`.
The condition goes in parentheses. The body goes in curly braces `{ … }`. Indent inside the braces — JavaScript doesn't care about indentation, but humans reading the code do.

lessons.task: Set `let age = 20;`. Print 'adult' if age is at least 18, otherwise print 'minor'.
lessons.starterCode:
let age = 20;
// if age >= 18 print 'adult', otherwise print 'minor'

lessons.title: 8. else if & logical operators
lessons.summary: Chaining decisions and combining conditions.

teaching.text: When you have more than two options, chain them with `else if`. The first matching branch runs; the rest are skipped.
You can also combine conditions: `&&` means both must be true, `||` means at least one must be true, `!` flips true↔false.

lessons.task: Set `let score = 75;`. Print 'A' if score ≥ 90, 'B' if ≥ 80, 'C' if ≥ 70, otherwise 'F'.
lessons.starterCode:
let score = 75;
// Print the letter grade using if / else if / else
hints: Start with the highest threshold first.
Check `>= 90`, then `>= 80`, then `>= 70`, then fall through to else.

lessons.title: 9. while loops
lessons.summary: Repeat as long as a condition holds.

teaching.text: A `while` loop runs its body again and again, as long as the condition stays true.
If the condition never becomes false, the loop runs forever. Always make sure something inside the body moves you toward the exit.

lessons.task: Use a while loop to print the numbers 1 through 5, each on its own line.
lessons.starterCode: // Print 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 — one per line — using while
hints: Start a counter at 1. Loop while it's <= 5. Print it. Add 1 each pass.
let i = 1; while (i <= 5) { console.log(i); i = i + 1; }

lessons.title: 10. for loops
lessons.summary: A while loop with the counter built in.

teaching.text: A `for` loop packs three things into one line: where the counter starts, how long to keep going, and how to step.
Read the header as: 'start with `i = 1`; while `i <= 3`; after each pass do `i = i + 1`'. You can step by anything — `i = i + 2` jumps two at a time.
`i++` is shorthand for `i = i + 1`. You'll see it everywhere.

lessons.task: Use a for loop to print the even numbers from 2 to 10 inclusive, one per line.
lessons.starterCode: // 2, 4, 6, 8, 10 — one per line
hints: Start at 2, go up to 10, step by 2.
for (let i = 2; i <= 10; i = i + 2) { console.log(i); }

lessons.title: 11. Arrays — basics
lessons.summary: An ordered list of values.

teaching.text: An array is a list. It's written with square brackets, and items are separated by commas.
Position numbers (indices) start at **0**, not 1. So the first item is `fruits[0]`, the second is `fruits[1]`, and so on.

lessons.task: Given the fruits array, print the second fruit on one line, then the array's length on the next.
lessons.starterCode:
let fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry'];
// Print the second fruit, then print fruits.length

lessons.title: 12. Looping over an array
lessons.summary: Visit each item without managing an index yourself.

teaching.text: You can loop an array with a classic `for` and `arr[i]`, but `for…of` is cleaner — it hands you each item directly.
Read it as: 'for each `c` of `colors`, do this.' The variable name (`c`) is yours to choose.

lessons.task: Loop over the fruits array and print each fruit on its own line.
lessons.starterCode:
let fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry'];
// Print each fruit on its own line

lessons.title: 13. Functions — declare, call, return
lessons.summary: A reusable mini-program with a name.

teaching.text: A function is a chunk of code with a name. You *declare* it once, then *call* it whenever you need it.

The bit in parentheses (`n`) is a **parameter** — a placeholder for whatever value the caller passes in. `return` is the function's answer. Whatever you return becomes the value of the call expression.
Without `return`, the function still runs, but calling it gives back `undefined` instead of a useful value.

lessons.task: Write a function `square(n)` that returns n × n. Call it with 7 and print the result.
lessons.starterCode:
// function square(n) { … }
// console.log(square(7));
hints: function square(n) { return n * n; } console.log(square(7));

lessons.title: 14. Objects — basics
lessons.summary: Bundling related values under one name.

teaching.text: An object groups related values together as **key → value** pairs. Curly braces, commas between pairs, colons between keys and values.
Read a value with dot notation: `user.name`. Add or change one the same way: `user.age = 26;`.

lessons.task: Given the user object, print: Alex is 25 — using a template literal.
lessons.starterCode:
let user = { name: 'Alex', age: 25 };
// Print: Alex is 25
lessons.solution:
let user = { name: 'Alex', age: 25 };
console.log(`${user.name} is ${user.age}`);

tracks.name: JavaScript — Functions Deep Dive
tracks.description: Parameters, returns, scope, and reusable logic.

lessons.title: 15. Calling a function
lessons.summary: A function does nothing until you call it.

teaching.text: A function is a reusable block of code. But declaring a function only teaches JavaScript what the function is. It does not run the function yet.
To call a function, write its name followed by parentheses: `sayHi()`.
Forgetting the parentheses is a common beginner bug. `sayHi` means the function itself. `sayHi()` runs it.

lessons.task: Create a function named `sayHello` that prints `Hello from a function!`, then call it.
hints: Put `console.log('Hello from a function!');` inside the function body.
After the closing brace, call it with `sayHello();`

lessons.title: 16. Function parameters
lessons.summary: A parameter lets a function receive a value.

teaching.text: A parameter is a name that stands in for a value passed into a function.
When you call `greet('Sam')`, JavaScript puts `'Sam'` into the `name` parameter for that one call.

lessons.task: Write a function `greet(name)` that prints `Hi, NAME!`. Call it with `Sam` and then with `Alex`.
hints: Call the function twice, once with `'Sam'` and once with `'Alex'`.

lessons.title: 17. Two parameters
lessons.summary: Functions can receive more than one value.

teaching.text: A function can have multiple parameters. Separate them with commas.
Arguments match parameters by position: the first argument goes into the first parameter, the second argument goes into the second parameter.

lessons.task: Write a function `add(a, b)` that prints the sum. Call it with `5` and `7`, then with `10` and `20`.
hints: The function header is `function add(a, b) {`.

lessons.title: 18. Returning a value
lessons.summary: `return` gives a value back to the code that called the function.

teaching.text: Printing and returning are different. `console.log()` shows a value in the console. `return` sends a value back from the function.
A returned value can be stored, printed, compared, or passed into another function.

lessons.task: Write a function `triple(n)` that returns `n * 3`. Print `triple(4)` and `triple(10)`.

lessons.title: 19. return vs console.log
lessons.summary: Use `return` when the caller needs the answer later.

teaching.text: A function that only logs a value shows the answer, but the rest of your program cannot reuse that answer.
Use `return` when the function should produce a value. Use `console.log()` when you want to display something.

lessons.task: Write `makeFullName(first, last)` so it returns the full name. Store the result for `Ada` and `Lovelace` in `name`, then print `name`.

lessons.title: 20. Calling a function multiple times
lessons.summary: One function can solve the same kind of problem many times.

teaching.text: The point of a function is reuse. Write the logic once, then call it with different inputs.
Each call gets its own value for the parameter.

lessons.task: Write `multiplyByTen(n)` that returns `n * 10`. Print the results for `3`, `8`, and `12`.
lessons.starterCode:
// Write multiplyByTen(n)
// Print multiplyByTen(3), multiplyByTen(8), multiplyByTen(12)

lessons.title: 21. Function expressions
lessons.summary: A function can be stored in a variable.

teaching.text: A function declaration starts with the `function` keyword. A function expression stores a function in a variable.
After the function is stored, you call it through the variable name: `double(9)`.

lessons.task: Create a function expression named `half` that returns `n / 2`. Print `half(10)` and `half(25)`.
lessons.starterCode:
// const half = function(n) { ... };
// Print half(10) and half(25)
hints: Do not forget the semicolon after the function expression.

lessons.title: 22. Arrow functions
lessons.summary: Arrow functions are a shorter way to write function expressions.

teaching.text: Arrow functions are commonly used in modern JavaScript. They are often shorter than regular function expressions.
For now, treat arrow functions as another way to write small reusable functions. Later, they become especially useful with arrays and events.

lessons.task: Create an arrow function named `subtract` that returns `a - b`. Print `subtract(10, 3)` and `subtract(20, 8)`.
lessons.starterCode:
// const subtract = (a, b) => { ... };
// Print two calls

lessons.title: 23. Default parameters
lessons.summary: A parameter can have a fallback value.

teaching.text: A default parameter gives JavaScript a value to use when the caller does not pass one.
Defaults make functions safer because missing arguments do not always break your output.

lessons.task: Write `welcome(name = 'guest')` that returns `Welcome, NAME`. Print `welcome('Mia')` and `welcome()`.
hints: Put the default value in the parameter list: `name = 'guest'`.
The second call should pass no arguments.

lessons.title: 24. Local variables inside functions
lessons.summary: Variables created inside a function belong to that function.

teaching.text: A variable created inside a function is local to that function. It exists while the function is running.
Local variables are useful for naming intermediate steps in a calculation.

lessons.task: Write `finalPrice(price)` that creates a local variable `shipping` set to `5`, then returns `price + shipping`. Print the final price for `20` and `100`.
